sentences.text: My complete name home in Poland was [?
Chaya ?] Estera Niesenhaus, and I was born in Poland, in Lodz, August 16, 1908.
My married name is Fox, F-O-X.
I lived with my parents, and I had two brothers [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until everything changed.
No.
There were aunts and uncles, but in the same city, but we didn't live together.
I never knew and never saw any grandparents.
My parents come from Russia, so it was a little different.
They didn't survive.
They never mentioned, I never asked.
An apartment building.
Yeah, always was a maid.
He was a merchant.
It was really together with the apartment apart.
In the same house together.
A housewife.
Religious, but I would say more traditional.
Also, it was kosher in my-- I remember, I know my father was praying every day.
Yeah, and the [? tallus ?] is--
Yes, yes, yes.
Yeah, I wasn't allowed to write, to-- to write and to-- to do anything on Saturday.
No.
Jewish and Polish.
Yiddish and Polish.
Yes, ?] Yiddish and Polish.
Yes.
Only Jewish children.
Polish, I think.
I did not.
I don't know whether it's because Russian, a girl is not supposed to-- to go.
They didn't ask, and I didn't go.
If they would ask, I probably would have had to go, but they never asked.
I think this is more a girl this wasn't important, only a boy.
On the holidays, the big holidays.
I don't remember as children, but later they were in school in gymnasium, so one was in the Russian gymnasium, and he was going on Saturday to school.
In certain, I will mention, the special condition, but I don't think so they were going.
Only on the big holidays.
Yes, it happened to be a Jewish area.
Yes, yes.
Yeah.
No, no.
This was a particular kind of business.
They imported things from Russia, and this was specific there in the town, so they came, private people.

It was laces and-- and ribbons and special tablecloths which made in Russia in a monastery, and my mother used to go once or twice a year to collect whatever they did, and this for special people came when they made a dowry, the-- the outfit for the bride.
So this was-- and it was not wholesale.
A specialty, but a high specialty.
Yeah, and-- and-- and Yiddish, but I don't remember, probably they were all kind here.
No.
Yes.
In business, but I never had anything to do with non-Jews.
Because later on, when I grew up, I was in a Jewish gymnasium, so I didn't mingle very much.
Some were not Jewish, but it were mostly Jewish.
It was a Jewish gymnasium, no Saturday school.
And in Europe, it's six days over Sunday, school.
No.
In Poland was no co-ed.
I don't think so there ever was.
But this school for sure was not.
Sometimes I walk.
Most of the time, there was a tram to go, a tram.
Yeah--
--even from the same building, we used to go together.
One waited for the other.
I think that's children.
8 O'clock.
No.
We took along a sandwich, a roll, whatever.
There was no cafeteria in those days, and there was nothing to drink, and I don't think-- I never took anything to drink.
How it was, I don't know.
And we were back at about 2:30.
And in Europe, the main meal is in the daytime.
So when I came home was the main meal.
A regular meal.
Soup, meat, and dessert, and that's all
Yeah, it was a medium bourgeois, not rich, but not poor.
I didn't grow up too much home, because I left for to study.
When I was a child, I didn't read paper.
And when I was at school, I was too busy, and I was not affiliated with no political or other organization, so I was limited.
I was a very serious student.
No, no.
I just said, I was not.
Yeah, there was a radio.
There was a telephone later, when it came out, yeah.
Polish, for sure.
Yiddish.
They spoke, among themselves, Yiddish.
From Russia, and my father too.
No, not among themselves.
Dinner, you mean.
I had homework.
We made homework till 10 o'clock in the evening.
There was never enough time.
There was no time for playing or such things.
No.
Occasionally, we went to a movie.
It was always serious.
Six, seven times, so this was the day when we-- the old girls, was just the same to go to a movie, but that's all.
Sometimes I went to a theatre when I grew up a little, when I was older.
I walked.
It depends.
Yeah, sure, it was a big city.
Lodz is the second big city after the Warsaw, which is the capital.
Polish.
No, not too much in groups.
I had one girlfriend, another, we were going together, yeah.
Sometimes, yeah, but only on a-- on a Saturday, because we had homework.
Was a lot of homework.
And I had music lessons, I played the piano, so I had to-- to-- I read.
There wasn't like now, let's say, to-- to play, to-- maybe other did, but I'm talking about myself.
Some, yes.
Later I went to a conservatory.
I wasn't good in music.
I wasn't interested, but my mother wanted, you know, but--
No, they would be one-- the younger took the violin also, not because we were musical so interested, but my mother wanted it.
It was status, probably.
No, no, no.
Jewish history, but we didn't pay too much-- and not we, the teacher.
He was-- a-- I-- the whole school, the personnel were mostly Bundists.
But when I was in school, I didn't know about it.
They didn't brainwash us, they didn't influence us, but the teacher of Jewish history wasn't so much interested we should learn Jewish history, that we should know Jewish literature.
And he had two hours a week, so one hour was history, which we didn't pay too much attention, and he didn't demand too much.
But the other, he read to us, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
And this now, the 'Fiddler on the Roof,' I know from his reading.
And also, the tendency in this school was more not to be too much Jewish in talking and not assimilated.

It was a Jewish gymnasium, but we were very happy to listen to him and begged him to read during the pause, when we were supposed to eat, the other 10, 15 minutes included in the 45 minutes which was the hour.
And let's say, the 'Fiddler on the Roof,' I know from original, but he read to us, and other things.
And this what I know a little  of the Jewish literature.
I know how to read too, but I don't read.
Yiddish, original, he read to us.
Everything Polish.
And German.
Was German, and we had Latin and such things.
So this is as it was, but everything was Polish.
No.
There was a Hebrew class too, but we didn't pay too much attention.
The teacher, I think, was more afraid of us than we of her.
That time wasn't so much importance, in our school, at least, because this wasn't especially-- they didn't brainwash us, but the atmosphere was they didn't pay so much attention to that.
No, no, no.
No prayer, no nothing.
No religious.
What?
No, no, no, it wasn't by selections.
Was people who could pay, and there were-- I think there were a few who got some scholarship paid less if they showed that they can't afford it.
But I wasn't in this category.
My parents never asked for a reduction in the fee.
Until I finished.
I entered at the age of eight, and I finished at the age of 17, the gymnasium.
And this what we call to get a Matura, yeah.
I graduated, I always knew that I want to do more education.
So I wanted to study medicine, but as my parents were of Russian origin, they themselves were not Polish citizens.
I wasn't yet, and 17, I was considered a minor.
Of course, I was born there.
Where?
The street?
Yeah, I-- I was a boarder with another classmate of mine.
Together, we went.
Classmate from school.
She went-- she remained and finished over there in Krakow.
I stayed only one year, and I didn't want to go back, by no means, for many reasons.
I wasn't so mature that I knew exact.
I didn't want to be-- they-- they-- the Poles, they were too blonde for me and too non-Jewish, and I-- they didn't do to me anything wrong.
No, actually, excuse me, they did a lot of wrong.
Not to me, personally.
I didn't want to stay anymore in Poland.
I wanted to go.
Yes, yes.
No, no, no, no.
It is quite far, yeah.
By train, I imagine.
Yeah, we both went.
No, no, no, I had nothing to do with them.
We ate outside.
Maybe breakfast, I don't remember how it was.
But lunch we ate, there was a mess hall for students, so we ate over there or outside.
And I don't remember exact, but nothing to do with the family.
My parents gave me the money, and I paid them, the same that my-- my classmate.
I don't remember, but maybe.
I don't think so.
Yes.
I passed some examination.
I remember, chemistry, I liked very much.
Physics, and botany, and theology, which I did not care too much about.
So this year, what was in the curriculum, I took.
No.
No.
Very little.
I mean, there-- there were, but I didn't date, no.
There were plenty of women, yeah.
In Poland were plenty of women.
Yeah.
They knew.
They looked at me, they knew.
The professor, they looked at me, my name was-- and my looks.
No, but I want to tell how it was in Krakow.
There was a lot of anti-Semitism at the University, because it didn't affect me personally, but it was.

Because a few subjects which I took, botany and zoology, were also included in the studying of agriculture, which the young sons of the nobility, the [? landholders, ?] had to take these courses, because others, it wasn't so important.
But they were very nationalistic and probably anti-Semitic, so they were seated on one side, and the Jews were sitting on the left side, separate.
Probably was ordered, but we revolted against this, so we were not sitting, we were standing.
I remember this, because I was only 17, but I knew.
But there was no beating this, I think.
But in Warsaw, at the same period of time, I know they were going worse than this sitting or standing.
They were fighting and [INAUDIBLE] and blood was running from the heads.
No, we didn't mingle with them.
They didn't mingle with us.
But I say that we had to sit on the left side, like outcasts.
And so we-- so a protest was, how could you protest?
We were standing.
I was only one year.
It was not all the subjects.
It was not all subjects the same.
Probably I wrote.
I don't remember.
There wasn't so much of telephone calls, no, not that time, I think.
Probably, yeah.
I was.
When I came home, I know I was nervous.
All right, but I was young.
By train, yeah.
I came home, and at that time, I became a citizen.
And my husband-- my father helped me to get all the papers, because my mother wasn't so very happy that I want to make such a move.
She was afraid if I go away, and I'm only 18 that time, the possibility was the choice to go to study medicine in France.
I could-- people were going to Vienna, but from our schools, and girls mostly, went to France.
In Poland?
Lodz, first of all, was not a university.
But the faculty wasn't medicine.
It wasn't for girls too much, and for Jews, for sure not.
There was only numerous classes.
It meets only 10% of Jewish population, so 10% eventually could be admitted to medical school.
So these were all very rich people who give donations or bribe, or the father was a doctor or somehow.
So this was out of the question.
And I, personally, wouldn't even want to be there.
I didn't want to see the faces, how they hated us, looking at us and treating us.
So I was young, and I was rebelling.
Yeah, sure.
I knew there were political, other excesses against Jews when there was independence of Poland, 1918.
There came nationalistic movements, so there were pogroms and other things, and I remembered that.
Also, I was never, ever hurt by anybody, hit by anybody, but others were hit, other Jews who were wearing the-- the-- the religious thing.
But not I, personally, but I was aware of what's going on.
I didn't talk so much about this, but I knew it, and everybody felt the same way.
Everybody felt it.
There wasn't like now, you sit with parents and discuss, but they felt it too.
I beg your pardon?
Sure, they felt it.
They felt it double, too.
As Russian Jews, they felt the-- the non-- the Orthodox, the local Jews thought that they are not Jews because they were not so religious.
And in appearance, [LAUGHS] they thought they are half Jews or not Jews at all, so they have to struggle with them too, but it is just nothing.
Yes.
Yeah.
So when the time came, I was admitted over there.
No, the [NON-ENGLISH] Matura, this was enough to be admitted.
Later, when I was as a student, I had to pass examination, but entry was no.
Yeah, yeah, sure.
I didn't run away.
[LAUGHS]
Maybe not.
Later, she-- she was proud, on one hand, but she was a Jewish mother, so it's understood.
Who, I?
Very little.
We had, in school, French, but you know, you know.
I came to France, I mixed up bonjour and au revoir.
I mixed it up.
But it came with gradually.
Well, a couple hours.
I don't know.
I know that my mother packed me a lot of food.
And that train, I always, later, when I came for vacation, I did two things on the trains, I ate and slept.
[LAUGHS]
The first time, I don't remember.
I think I went with a girlfriend from my class, which she went also to study, but I'm not so sure.
I think so.
And-- yeah, yeah.
And we came to Paris together, and we were afraid.
We took-- they told us where to go, the section for students, and we took a [INAUDIBLE], [? droshka, ?] and we were afraid maybe it would take us somewhere else.
So we were-- we were shivering, and the Matura, we were holding here, in-- under the blouse.
This was the most precious things which we were holding on, and we had whatever the luggage.
You know, he did the-- we thought, who knows where he takes us, but he took us.
Huh?
No.
It was a horse and buggy, yeah.
That time, were no cars.
So we spoke French, yeah.
No, the-- there were no dormitory.
In Paris, in those times, students lived in hotels in the Quartier Latin, in the Quarter, not because it's the Latin Quarter for amusement, but over there was the medical school and the Sorbonne.
So-- so we-- I don't remember how-- how-- what we told him.

And we went from one little hotel to another to find a room, and they knew that student-- in the beginning, they treated us with suspicion, because in France, you know, a girl had to have a chaperone, and they watched her, and here, are all of a sudden, come two young kids.
Two young kids.
And they-- we-- we assume that, so they thought we are run away, outlaws, which parent let go.
So they had suspicions.
But with the time, they learned.
They learned, or rather before I came, there were others, so they had already an idea that this isn't so bad.
[LAUGHS]
No, probably my father provided that we should have the French currency available, that we have to go to a bank to change.
He probably changed it there.
I don't remember.
It was-- it is 70 years ago.
Wait, yeah.
To the faculty.
Yeah, to the faculty, yeah.
And in France, I have to say, I didn't have all the years.
It took eight years, through '33 from '26, so seven years.
I didn't have any difficulty of the question being Jewish.
It never came out.
They never asked, I never said.
I don't know whether they were so aware.
When I came to France, I thought everybody's Jewish, because they are dark, they are short, and they are gesticulate.
I thought they are Jews.
But what I want to emphasize, they are not too friendly to foreigners.
No students, girl or boy, eventually, invited to a home, not even Jewish.
But they are against foreigners.
But I didn't feel any difficulties in this respect.
We didn't meet too much.
Not only I, none of us.
Because the French, they don't-- the students, they are poor.
They all think they are poor.
Doesn't mean that everybody was so poor, but they don't socialize with foreigners.
So that's all.
Among students-- among students-- I socialized mostly with Polish.
Yeah.
From all over the country.
Not from my town, necessarily.
Not-- nobody.
I don't remember now.
But we kept together.
They wanted Polish girl, they have, maybe on the side, their own acquaintances, but we were colleagues.
Yeah.
Yes.
Outside.
Breakfast, we made-- we had a little alcohol, little burner with a [INAUDIBLE] but we eat nothing.
[LAUGHS] I don't remember.
But we ate outside.
We couldn't cook in the room.
I don't remember.
Probably the-- the laundry.
Not to wash yourself.
You know.
There was no bathroom at all.
Even the toilet, this is-- the Latin Quarter is old-- was in the lobby, and everything, it was a walk-up, and going up the stairs.
This is old [LAUGHS] stuff, you know, I don't think of it ever.
I don't know how we survived this.
But everybody was the same things, not only I.
For vacation, for the summer.
By train, yes.
Yeah.
No, no, yeah, no.
Oh, I forgot to say, oh, that I forgot altogether now.
I went to France with my brother, an older brother, who went to study also, and-- and he-- oh, I forgot, I mixed up a little bit.
We went first to Nancy.
I was admitted to the faculty of Nancy, and he was there to study agriculture, and I, medicine.
But being there, I found out that north in Normandy, in a town, Rouen, they give credit for these who studied abroad.
And I was one year in Krakow, so I changed everything, and I went to Rouen with the idea that maybe they will give me credit for the year I was in Krakow, but it didn't work out.
So I did the first year medicine, which is really only preparation to medicine, the entrance, the [NON-ENGLISH] science natural.
So over there, I did that in Rouen, and when I finished Rouen, I went to Paris.
That was your brother Michael?
No, Isak.
Michael was home.
We  didn't talk about him.
